categories.title: BODY OF KNOWLEDGE
categories.theme: Facts about the human body and biology

clues.clue: The human heart is divided into this many chambers, two atria and two ventricles.
The biggest distinction between a eukaryotic cell and a prokaryotic cell is the presence or absence of this organelle.
Unlike the left lung, which has two lobes, the human right lung has this many lobes.
This soft, purplish organ filters blood and stores platelets, and is located in the upper left abdomen.
This copper-rich protein is responsible for the blue blood found in the Antarctic octopus.

categories.title: PEAKS & LEGENDS
categories.theme: Famous mountains and their cultural or historical significance

clues.clue: Vatican City, the world's smallest country, is fully enclosed within this nation, home to the Colosseum and the Sistine Chapel.
Ancient Greeks believed this mountain, home of the gods, was the center of the Earth.
This dormant volcano is the highest peak in Turkey and is deeply tied to the country's national identity, even appearing on its coat of arms.
The Danube River, flowing through ten European countries, empties into this body of water.
Archaeologists consider this ancient city in the West Bank — whose name means 'City of the Moon' in Arabic — to be the world's oldest walled city.

categories.title: SCREEN LEGENDS
categories.theme: Actors and iconic roles in film history

clues.clue: This actor played Bruce Wayne and his caped alter ego in Tim Burton's 1989 blockbuster 'Batman'.
The Shawshank Redemption, widely regarded as one of the greatest films ever made, was released in this year.
This Spanish actress, known for her collaborations with director Pedro Almodóvar, starred in both 'Volver' and 'All About My Mother'.
Peter Cushing portrayed Baron Victor Frankenstein in this 1957 Hammer horror film.
Frances McDormand took home the Academy Award for Best Leading Actress in 2021 for playing this character in the film 'Nomadland'.

categories.title: DOT COM GEOGRAPHY
categories.theme: Country-code top-level domains (.xx) matched to their nations

clues.clue: The country-code domain '.ru' belongs to Russia; this Southeast Asian nation claims '.th' as its own.
Web addresses ending in '.nl' belong to this Western European nation, home to Amsterdam and the Rijksmuseum.
The country-code top-level domain '.sa' is registered to this oil-rich Middle Eastern kingdom.
Alberta is a Canadian province, NOT an Australian state — this continent's territories use domains like '.au', while Alberta's country uses '.ca'.
The capital of this island nation off the southeast coast of Africa is Antananarivo, one of the longest capital city names in the world.

categories.title: FOOD FOR THOUGHT
categories.theme: Culinary facts about dishes, ingredients, and drinks

clues.clue: This classic candy's marketing slogan invites you to 'Taste the rainbow.'
Marzipan, the confection used in pastries and sculpted decorations, is made primarily from these ground nuts.
This refreshing yogurt-based drink — often blended with fruit, spices, or rose water — originated in India.
Scotland is the country most closely associated with this amber spirit, distilled from malted grain and aged in oak barrels.
This Scandinavian delicacy consists of raw salmon cured without heat in a mixture of salt, sugar, and dill.

categories.title: THAT'S ITALIAN!
categories.theme: Things associated with Italy or the Italian language

clues.clue: The legendary sports cars of this brand, including the F40 and Testarossa, are manufactured in Maranello, Italy.
This Italian grape-based spirit, often sipped after dinner, is distilled from the skins and seeds left over from winemaking.
In Italian, this single word means the color 'green,' and is also familiar to English speakers as the name of a popular coffee chain's color palette.

This UNESCO-listed Berber oasis town in Libya, known for its ancient mud-brick architecture, shares its name with a region of North Africa long tied to trans-Saharan trade — but it sits in Libya, not Italy, unlike the desert drink that inspired many Italian travelers.
Despite widespread belief, the Italian word for 'blue' is spelled this way — often misspelled by English speakers who assume it matches the French 'azur.'

categories.title: OSCAR GOLD
categories.theme: Academy Award winners for acting roles

clues.clue: Contrary to popular belief, Leonardo DiCaprio did NOT win an Oscar for Best Actor for his role in the 2004 Howard Hughes biopic 'The Aviator.'
This actress took home the 2016 Best Supporting Actress Oscar for her powerful portrayal of Rose Maxson opposite Denzel Washington in the film adaptation of August Wilson's 'Fences.'
He won the 2018 Best Supporting Actor Oscar for his role as classical pianist Dr. Donald Shirley in 'Green Book,' a film that also took home Best Picture.
This actor claimed the 2008 Best Leading Actor Oscar for his portrayal of slain San Francisco politician Harvey Milk in Gus Van Sant's biographical film.
At the 2023 ceremony, the Oscars broke over 60 years of tradition when this famous pre-show walkway was changed from its iconic red color to a champagne hue.
clues.answer: The red carpet changed to a champagne carpet

categories.title: ROYAL HEADACHES
categories.theme: British monarchs and their historical moments

clues.clue: A classic mnemonic helps students remember the fates of Henry VIII's six wives in order: Divorced, Beheaded, Died, Divorced, Beheaded, and this final fate.
This king of England, who died in 1547, is remembered for his six marriages and was survived by his final wife, Catherine Parr.
This British monarch sat on the throne during the entire American War of Independence from 1775 to 1783.
The famous exasperated phrase 'Will no one rid me of this turbulent priest?' — reportedly leading to the murder of Archbishop Thomas Becket — is attributed to this medieval English king.
Mussolini's Italy invaded this African nation in 1935, an act of aggression that weakened the League of Nations and drew global condemnation.

categories.title: SMALL BUT MIGHTY
categories.theme: Things that are smaller versions or smaller forms of something larger

clues.clue: This woodwind instrument is essentially a half-sized flute, pitched an octave higher, and is the highest-voiced instrument in a standard orchestra.
In mathematics, this single letter from the alphabet represents the imaginary unit — the square root of negative one.
This tiny island nation south of India is nicknamed the 'teardrop of India' for both its location off the subcontinent's southern tip and its distinctive shape.
Andorra is one of Europe's smallest nations, sandwiched between Spain and this other country that forms its northern border.
The domestic apple tree goes by this two-word Linnean scientific name, a far cry from the grand orchards it produces.
This short word is what the French word 'qui' translates to in English.
In Portuguese, the word 'quatro' means this number.
A 'diphthong' is the linguistic term for two vowel sounds joined in one syllable, as heard in the 'ou' of 'out' or the 'oy' of this word meaning a young male.
This adjective, meaning 'sad or miserable in appearance,' combines two smaller words and sounds like something straight out of a Victorian novel.
This verb, meaning 'to treat someone like a celebrity,' shares its root with the king of the jungle.

finals.title: CROWNING ACHIEVEMENT
finals.clue: On Christmas Day in the year 800 AD, this pope placed the imperial crown on the head of Charlemagne, establishing him as Holy Roman Emperor.

finals.title: FIRST IN THE CHEST
finals.clue: Performed in 1967, this medical milestone was the world's first successful operation of its kind — a surgeon replaced a dying patient's most vital organ with a donor's.

finals.title: SPAGHETTI SEQUENCE
finals.clue: Director Sergio Leone's 'Dollars Trilogy' consists of three Westerns released between 1964 and 1966. Name all three films in order of release to win.
finals.answer: A Fistful of Dollars, For a Few Dollars More, The Good the Bad and the Ugly

finals.title: SCIENCE DEEP CUTS
finals.clue: Autosomal-dominant Compelling Helio-Ophthalmic Outburst syndrome — mercifully acronymed ACHOO — is the scientific name for the uncontrollable urge to do this when suddenly exposed to bright sunlight.

finals.title: HISTORY'S REBELS
finals.clue: In early 19th-century England, the original members of this movement were not opponents of technology in general but were skilled craftsmen in this specific industry who smashed machinery that threatened their livelihoods — lending their name to modern anti-tech sentiment.
